name: Parc de Majolan et Réserve écologique des barails - loop around Lac de Bordeaux
name_original: Parc de Majolan et Réserve écologique des barails - boucle autour du Lac de Bordeaux
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around the Garonne-Kanal primarily follow the 270-kilometer Garonne Canal, connecting Bordeaux and Toulouse. The region is characterized by predominantly flat, car-free greenways, often shaded by plane trees, making it accessible for various skill levels. Cyclists encounter diverse landscapes including vineyards, orchards, agricultural lands, and wooded areas, with the canal running parallel to the Garonne River. The terrain offers a mix of tranquil waterways and rural scenery across southwest France. Best touring cycling routes around the Garonne-Kanal The most popular touring cycling route is Parc de Majolan et Réserve écologique des barails - loop around Lac de Bordeaux, a 19.3 mile (31.0 km) trail that takes 1 hour 54 minutes to complete. This easy route offers a scenic loop around Lac de Bordeaux. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Betren - Vielha - Es Bòrdes - Gausac - Escunhau — circular through Medio Arán, a difficult 19.4 mile (31.2 km) path. This route provides a challenging circular journey through the Medio Arán region. Local touring cyclists also love the Varradòs – cycle touring in Catalonia, a 28.9 mile (46.6 km) trail leading through Catalonia, often completed in about 4 hours 35 minutes. Touring cycling around the Garonne-Kanal is defined by shaded waterways, expansive vineyards, and charming historic towns. The network offers options for both leisurely rides on flat greenways and more challenging routes through varied terrain. The routes in Garonne canal are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 2,400 reviews. More than 27,000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Garonne canal's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around the Garonne Canal?
A: The Garonne Canal region offers a vast network of touring cycling routes, with over 1,500 options recorded on komoot. These routes cater to various skill levels, including nearly 700 easy routes, over 500 moderate routes, and more than 300 challenging paths.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see while cycling along the Garonne Canal?
A: Cyclists along the Garonne Canal will experience diverse landscapes. The route is famously shaded by majestic plane trees lining the tranquil waterways. You'll also pass through renowned wine regions like Entre-deux-Mers, Gaillac, and Cahors, featuring expansive vineyards and plum orchards. Beyond these, the path traverses peaceful agricultural lands, wooded areas, and rolling hills, showcasing the rich rural scenery of southwest France. Don't miss the impressive aqueduct at Agen, carrying the canal over the Garonne River.

Q: Are there any family-friendly cycling routes along the Garonne Canal?
A: Yes, the Garonne Canal is ideal for family cycling. The path offers a predominantly flat and secure experience, primarily along paved greenways that are free from car traffic. Many sections are shaded, providing a comfortable ride for all ages and skill levels. An excellent option for families is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e961496580/parc-de-majolan-et-reserve-ecologique-des-barails-loop-around-lac-de-bordeaux" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parc de Majolan et Réserve écologique des barails - loop around Lac de Bordeaux&lt;/a&gt;, an easy route offering a scenic loop.

Q: What are some notable attractions or landmarks to explore near the cycling routes?
A: The Garonne Canal region is rich in cultural and natural attractions. You can visit historic cities like Bordeaux, a UNESCO World Heritage site with landmarks such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/929964" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bordeaux Water Mirror&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5873675" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pont de Pierre (Stone Bridge)&lt;/a&gt;. Further along, Moissac boasts a magnificent 12th-century Abbey, also a UNESCO site. You'll also find charming medieval bastide towns and the impressive Agen aqueduct. For natural beauty, consider visiting nearby lakes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3302745" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lake Major of Colomers&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4645821" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bordeaux lake&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there circular cycling routes available in the Garonne Canal area?
A: Yes, there are many circular routes for touring cyclists. These loops allow you to start and end your ride in the same location, offering convenience. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e967362332/betren-vielha-es-bordes-gausac-escunhau-circular-through-medio-aran" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Betren - Vielha - Es Bòrdes - Gausac - Escunhau — circular through Medio Arán&lt;/a&gt; provides a challenging circular journey, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e929428469/cazeres-garonne-cycle-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cazères Garonne Cycle Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a moderate option.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in the Garonne Canal region?
A: The touring cycling routes around the Garonne Canal are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average rating of 4.3 stars from over 2,400 reviews. Cyclists often praise the peaceful, car-free greenways, the refreshing shade provided by the plane trees, and the diverse scenery that ranges from vineyards to historic towns. Over 27,000 touring cyclists have already used komoot to explore this varied terrain.

Q: Can I access the Garonne Canal cycling routes using public transport?
A: While the Garonne Canal connects major cities like Bordeaux and Toulouse, which are well-served by public transport, specific access points along the canal for cyclists with bikes can vary. It's advisable to check local train or bus services for their bike carriage policies and nearest stops to your desired starting point. Many sections of the canal are easily accessible from towns and villages along its length.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay along the Garonne Canal cycling route?
A: Yes, the Garonne Canal path passes through numerous charming towns and villages where you can find cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options. From historic bastide towns to larger cities like Agen and Moissac, there are ample opportunities to sample local gastronomy, visit markets, and find places to rest. It's recommended to plan your stops in advance, especially during peak season.

Q: What is the best time of year for a touring cycling trip along the Garonne Canal?
A: The Garonne Canal is enjoyable for touring cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery, with fewer crowds. During warmer months, the abundant shade from the plane trees makes cycling comfortable, but it can still get quite warm. Always check the local weather forecast before you set out.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the Garonne Canal bike paths?
A: Generally, dogs are welcome on the Garonne Canal bike paths, especially if kept on a leash. The greenways are popular for leisurely strolls and rides, making them suitable for well-behaved dogs. However, always be mindful of other users and local regulations, particularly in more populated areas or nature reserves. Ensure you carry water for your dog, especially on longer rides.

Q: Where can I find parking facilities if I drive to the Garonne Canal cycling routes?
A: Parking facilities are typically available in the towns and villages located along the Garonne Canal. Major access points, especially near popular attractions or larger towns like Agen, Moissac, or near Bordeaux and Toulouse, will have designated parking areas. Look for public car parks in these areas, often close to the canal path itself, to begin your cycling adventure.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes in the Garonne Canal region for experienced cyclists?
A: While the Garonne Canal path itself is largely flat, the broader region offers more challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1285597444/varrados-cycle-touring-in-catalonia" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Varradòs – cycle touring in Catalonia&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 28.9-mile (46.6 km) trail with significant elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e964107466/towns-of-the-val-d-aran" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Towns of the Val d'Aran&lt;/a&gt;, which also presents a difficult challenge with its varied terrain.
tips: The park of Majolan. Classified "remarkable garden". Created at the end of the 19th century by a wealthy banker from Bordeaux, it is a place for strolling and relaxation popular with all visitors. It has some of the largest artificial caves in Europe and its park conceals false ruins, remarkable bridges, a vast body of water, an island which serves as a nature reserve for birds. In short, a place to discover absolutely located 10 km from Bordeaux and free access.
It's always very pleasant to cross the ecological reserve and the floral park before finding "urban civilization" and the new districts of Jinko and Bassins à flots.
Pedestrian and cycle bridge 200 meters long which allows you to cross the Lake of Bordeaux. Breathtaking view of the lake.
